blocks.statement: I can explain why 2026-XJ's velocity won't stay constant, and I can name the force that bends its path.

blocks.whatWeKnow: NASA has refined 2026-XJ's orbit using three more weeks of radar tracking. Semi-major axis a ≈ 1.42 AU, eccentricity e ≈ 0.31, currently about 1.18 AU out — and closing.
blocks.connection: Unit 1 answered HOW FAST. Unit 2 answers WHY — and that means a force. Today we name that force and frame the unit's question: does the asteroid's path bend enough to bring it to Earth?

blocks.title: NASA / Planetary Defense Briefing — Update 2
blocks.markdown:
The earlier Unit 1 prediction assumed the asteroid's velocity stayed constant. **It does not.** Gravity bends its path. The new orbit solution treats 2026-XJ as a Sun-orbiting body governed by Newton's law of universal gravitation.

The new question: where does its orbit **cross Earth's**? And — load-bearing — will both objects arrive at the crossing at the **same time**? Unit 2 builds the physics to answer this.

*This is fiction. The physics — and the equations — are real.*

## What makes a path bend?

Newton's 1st Law (Unit 1): with **no net force**, an object keeps moving in a straight line at constant velocity. Forever.

2026-XJ is **not** moving in a straight line — radar shows its path curving around the Sun. By Newton's own logic, something must be pulling on it.

That something is **gravity** — mostly the Sun's, and (much more weakly) Earth's. This unit is about how that pull works, how strong it is at any distance, and what kind of path it produces.

blocks.instruction: Predict before the demo runs: a book and a FLAT sheet of paper are dropped together; then a book and a CRUMPLED sheet of paper. Sketch the race(s), circle which one(s) you think hit first, and write WHY next to your sketch.
prompts: Round 1: book vs. flat paper — who wins?
Round 2: book vs. crumpled paper — who wins?
Write your WHY in a corner of the canvas.

blocks.title: In class today — the drop races
blocks.markdown: We ran the races live: book vs. flat paper, then book vs. crumpled paper. The flat sheet drifts down late; the crumpled one essentially **ties the book**. Air resistance — not gravity — made the difference. Run the vacuum-chamber simulation below and watch what happens when air is removed entirely.

blocks.patternPrompt: What did you NOTICE in the drop races (live or in the simulation)?
blocks.interpretPrompt: What do you WONDER about gravity after watching them?

blocks.prompt: Write your ONE question about gravity — the thing you most want this unit to answer.
blocks.frame: My one question about gravity is ___.

blocks.prompt: Why doesn't 2026-XJ just drift off into space at constant velocity? What pulls on it?
blocks.frame: It can't keep a constant velocity because ___ is pulling on it, so its path ___.
